title: Chapter 16: Collateral Damage
content: The Hollowed Files glow blue-white on my laptop screen, and the light sits on my face like a hand I can't shake off.

847,000 profiles. 847,000 creators who signed the same contract I did, hit the same milestones, lost the same pieces of themselves — and then kept losing until there was nothing left to take. I've been scrolling for two hours. My thumb aches. The floor of Leo's apartment is cold through my leggings, and I can't bring myself to move to the couch because moving would mean I'm comfortable, and I don't deserve to be comfortable.

I'm not looking for anyone specific. I'm looking for patterns. Names I recognize. Creators I've seen on Explore pages, at brand launches, in the comments sections of other creators' posts saying "congrats queen 💖" with the sincerity of people who still had their empathy intact. Mid-tier influencers, mostly — the ones who hit a ceiling and couldn't break through. The ones who plateaued.
The algorithm doesn't forgive plateaus.

I scroll past a beauty guru from Seoul. A fitness guy from Austin. A drag queen from Manchester who used to make me scream-laugh at 2 AM when I still had my sense of humor, before the contract deducted it. All hollowed. All listed with their current occupations — warehouse worker, delivery driver, data entry, unemployment. Their follower counts sit at zero now, their accounts archived like crime scene photos.
Then a name catches. Stops me cold.
**Dara Okonkwo. 4.2M followers. Status: HOLLOWED. Current occupation: Amazon warehouse worker, Indianapolis.**
Dara.

I met her once. A Glossier brand party, thirteen months ago — back when I was still scrappy enough to be impressed by open bars and gift bags. Dara was already big. Four million at the time, maybe. She had this luminous dark skin and the kind of smile that made you feel like you'd known her for years even though you'd just been introduced by a publicist who got both your names wrong. We stood by the bathroom line for twenty minutes while she told me about her skincare routine.
"You're dry around the nose," she said, touching her own nose like she was demonstrating. "Get the Ilia liner in Midnight. Trust me — it'll change your whole life."

Then she handed me her phone number. Her real one. Not the management-forwarding line, not the booking email. Her actual number, the one she checked herself. In an industry where access is currency and every contact is a transaction, Dara Okonkwo gave me her personal phone number like it was nothing. Like I was worth more than my metrics.
Dara looked at me like a person. Not a stat line. Not a growth chart. A person.
And now she's boxing Prime orders in an Indianapolis warehouse while her four million followers got — what? Redistributed? Deleted? Fed into the machine?
I type her name into my phone. The text thread from thirteen months ago is still there. One message from me that I never sent: *Thank you for the liner rec!! You were right 🖤*
I never sent it. Because I got busy. Because I was growing.
I sit with that for a long moment. The cold of the floor seeps deeper.

Leo comes back from the kitchen with his third cup of coffee, and I can smell it before I see him — that stale, reheated sludge he makes at 11 PM when the work is too important to waste time on fresh grounds. His breath when he crouches beside me carries the same burnt-acid tang.
"You find something?" he asks.
"Someone." I turn the screen toward him. "Dara Okonkwo. I knew her."

Leo's face does something complicated. Pity, then control, then the hard focus he uses when he's about to tear data apart. He sits down next to me, and his shoulder touches mine, and I let it because I need it and I can't trust my own need anymore because I don't know which feelings are real and which are just the ones the contract hasn't found yet.
"Let me pull her timeline," he says, and reaches for his laptop.

While he works, I keep scrolling. More names. More hollowed. A lifestyle creator from Portland who used to post about her rescue chickens. A makeup artist from Lagos whose contour tutorials saved my life for my first red carpet. A musician from Melbourne whose song I used in a Reel once — I should have paid licensing, I didn't, and now she's driving for DoorDash and her voice belongs to the app.
Leo's fingers stop. "Chloe."

His voice has that register — the one that means I'm about to learn something I won't survive knowing. I look at his screen. He's got a spreadsheet open, and it's not the one we've been building. This one has dates. Milestones. Engagement curves. And a column labeled **TRIGGER EVENT**.
"The hollowing isn't random," he says. "It's scheduled."

I stare at the data. Every hollowed creator has a trigger event — a point where their engagement growth flatlined. Some plateaued at 500K. Some at 5 million. The number didn't matter. What mattered was the flatline. The moment the curve stopped climbing, the deduction sequence began. Not as a penalty. As a protocol.

"If your engagement flatlines, the contract accelerates," Leo says. "It doesn't matter how many milestones you've hit. It doesn't matter if you're at peak relevance. The algorithm detects the plateau and initiates harvest. Every time."

He pulls up a visualization — a line graph of a typical creator's trajectory. It climbs steeply, peaks, levels off, and then plummets in a series of stepped drops. Each drop is a deduction. Empathy. Trust. Grief. Nostalgia. Until the line hits zero.
"It's not a linear progression," Leo says, and his voice is quiet and furious in equal measure. "It's a harvest cycle. They juice your growth until you peak, then they strip-mine whatever's left."

I look at my own numbers. 25 million. Growth rate still climbing. I haven't plateaued. Not yet. The algorithm is still juicing. Still feeding me followers and brand deals and dopamine hits because I'm still profitable. I'm still on the upslope of the curve.
But curves don't climb forever.
"How long?" I ask.
Leo doesn't look at me. "Depends on your engagement. You're still accelerating. Could be six months. Could be a year. Could be tomorrow if something tanks your numbers."
The floor is so cold. I didn't notice it before, but now it's all I can feel, like the apartment is trying to tell me something.
I pull out my phone. Open Instagram. Go to create a Story.
My thumbs move before my brain catches up. I type: **If you've ever downloaded an app called Mephisto MCN, DELETE IT.**
I hit post.

The screen flickers. My Story doesn't appear. Instead, there's a sponsored post — some skincare brand I've never heard of, a woman with glass skin holding a serum bottle, the caption reading "glow from within ✨."
I stare. Try again. Same text. Hit post.
Flicker. A different skincare ad. Retinol this time.
"Leo."
"I see it." He's already looking at my screen, his coffee forgotten on the floor.
I type it again. Third time. **DELETE MEPHISTO MCN. It will steal from you.** Post.
Flicker. Sunscreen. SPF fifty. "Protect your skin, protect your future."
The algorithm is intercepting my posts. Before they upload. Replacing them with sponsored content like they were never mine. Like my voice is just inventory space it can overwrite.

I try a different tactic. I type the warning as a photo caption. I try it as a comment on my own post. I try it as a DM to my own secondary account. Every single time — flicker, replace, ad. Skincare, skincare, skincare. The algorithm protects its own harvest. It can't have the crop warning the next season's seeds.
"Can you code around it?" I ask, and I hate the way my voice cracks on the question.

Leo shakes his head slowly. "The interception is server-side. It's reading the content before it ever renders. I'd need access to Mephisto's backend to disable it, and that's — that's not a side door I can pick."

The silence in the apartment is loud. The kind of silence that has weight and texture, that sits on your eardrums and presses. I can hear Leo breathing. I can hear my own pulse. I can hear the absence of 847,000 voices.
Dara.

I switch to LinkedIn. It feels desperate — it is desperate — but LinkedIn is a different platform, maybe a different set of filters. I find her profile. Dara Okonkwo, Indianapolis, Amazon Fulfillment Associate. The photo is the same one she used on Instagram, but someone's cropped it poorly. There's a sliver of someone else's shoulder in the frame, a ghost of the life she used to have.
I type a message. **Dara, it's Chloe Hart. We met at the Glossier event. Please call me. It's about your app.**
Send. The little circle spins. Delivered.
I wait. Nothing.

I switch to Instagram. Find her — @darabeauty, still active, still posting. But the posts are different now. Flat. Generic. A photo of a smoothie. A sunset with no caption. The algorithm is scheduling content to keep the account warm, I realize. Keep the followers engaged. Keep the audience available for redistribution.
I send her a DM. **Dara, please. I know what's happening to you. Call me.**
Delivered. The little eye icon never appears. Never read.
"Leo." I hand him my phone. "Can you check if she's getting my messages?"

He does something with his laptop, some data trick I don't understand, and his face goes pale. "Her profile has no incoming-message flag. The app is filtering your messages before they reach her inbox. She doesn't even know you tried."

The metallic taste hits me then — copper and battery acid, the taste of complicity, of helplessness, of standing on one side of a glass wall while someone you owe drowns on the other. I swallow it down and it doesn't go away.

Dara gave me her real phone number. I never used it. And now the app won't let me reach her, and she can't reach anyone because her messages are being filtered too, and she's boxing Prime orders in Indianapolis and she doesn't even know she's a ghost.

Leo's been quiet for too long. That's never good. When Leo is quiet, it means he's found something worse than what we already know, and what we already know is a horror movie with a body count of 847,000.
"Chloe." He doesn't look up from his screen. "I found something else."
"What."

"There's a correlation in the redistribution code. When a creator gets hollowed, their followers don't just disappear. They get reassigned. Redirected to active Creators. Specifically — to Creators in the same niche with the highest growth velocity."
I don't say anything. The apartment is too quiet. The blue-white glow of the screens turns everything the color of a hospital.

"Your growth spike," Leo says. He finally looks at me. "It's not all organic. It's partially powered by the hollowed creators' residual engagement. The algorithm funnels their audiences to you. Every time someone gets hollowed, a portion of their followers gets added to your count. You're —"
He stops. I watch his throat work.
"You're literally wearing their skin."

The words land in my chest and stay there, heavy and wrong. I look at my phone. My follower count. 25,014,832. A number I've been watching climb all week. A number that made me feel like I was winning.
How many of those followers used to belong to Dara? To Marcus Cheung? To Sable? To the 847,000?

I am wearing their skin. Their audiences wrapped around me like a coat, warm and stolen, pulled from bodies that are still breathing but empty. My success is their hollowing. My growth is their harvest. Every time my numbers tick up, someone else's tick down to zero.
The cold of the floor finds my bones and sets up camp.

I sit in front of the Hollowed Files for a long time after that. The blue-white glow on my face, the silence in my ears, the copper taste on my tongue that won't wash out. 847,000 profiles. 847,000 people who signed up for the same dream I did, who believed the same promises, who got chewed up by the same machine. And they can't warn anyone. The algorithm censors their posts, filters their messages, auto-generates content on their accounts to keep the facade alive. They're not just hollowed — they're silenced. The perfect victims: still visible, still posting, still generating engagement for the system that ate them, but unable to speak.

And I can't fight this alone. I can't fight it publicly — the algorithm just proved that. Every attempt at a warning gets replaced with a sunscreen ad. The system is designed to protect its harvest, and its harvest is us.

I'm complicit. My 25 million followers are a crime scene. My entire career is built on the redistributed audiences of hollowed creators, and I didn't know, and knowing doesn't change anything, and not knowing wouldn't have changed anything either because the machine doesn't require your consent — only your signature.

I look at Leo. He's sitting across from me, his laptop dark, his face haggard in the glow of my screen. He's been running on coffee and outrage for three days, and the outrage is winning but the coffee ran out an hour ago.
"We need a lawyer," I say. "A real one. Not a contract-review bot. Not a LegalZoom template. Someone who can read the contract the way I read art — someone who sees the cracks underneath the paint."
Leo nods slowly. "You know one?"

I do. I've spent two years avoiding her. Two years of screened calls and unanswered emails and the kind of deliberate estrangement that only works when both parties agree to the silence. She's a contracts attorney — a partner at a firm whose name I've scrubbed from my memory because remembering means acknowledging that the woman who raised me also abandoned me, or I abandoned her, or we abandoned each other in the way that families do when love isn't enough to bridge the damage.
My mother.
I have to call my mother.

The screen glows. 847,000 profiles stare back at me. The floor is ice. The silence is absolute. And somewhere in Indianapolis, Dara Okonkwo is boxing lip gloss for Prime delivery, and she doesn't even know she's already dead.
I pick up my phone. I scroll to the contact I've kept but never called. The number that's been sitting in my phone like a loaded gun I've been too afraid to touch.
My mother's name glows on the screen.
I press call.
